metadata.name: Sapphire Glass
metadata.title: Sapphire Glass Settings
metadata.description:
When laser cleaning sapphire glass, watch its extreme hardness first. It resists contaminants well but can crack under sudden heat. We've found that starting with gentle pulses avoids this. 

This material stands out for its heat resistance in tough spots like watches or medical gear. Low expansion keeps it stable, unlike softer glasses. We typically use steady scans to clear grime without scratching. 

Just adjust overlap for even coverage. Skip high speeds—they scatter debris. In our experience, this restores shine safely every time.

author.name: Ikmanda Roswati
author.title: Ph.D.

powerRange.description: Optimal power range for Sapphire Glass cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Optimal wavelength for Sapphire Glass processing with good absorption

spotSize.description: Optimal spot size for precision cleaning of Sapphire Glass surfaces

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

energyDensity.description: Optimal energy density for effective contaminant removal

pulseWidth.description: Optimal pulse width for controlled ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Optimal number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal overlap ratio between adjacent laser tracks

dwellTime.description: Optimal dwell time per cleaning spot location

power.description: Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG lasers at 1064 nm used in delicate substrate cleaning; starting at 50 W balances efficiency and safety for sapphire's high melting point (~2040°C) and low absorption at this wavelength, avoiding thermal damage (IPG Photonics laser cleaning systems specs; J. Laser Appl. 2018 study on oxide ceramics).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal; slower for heavy contaminants (e.g., oils), faster for light dust; 2000 mm/s recommended for uniform cleaning on sapphire without excessive heat buildup, considering its good thermal conductivity (industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; Opt. Lasers case studies).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred over picosecond/femtosecond for cost-effective industrial cleaning; 20 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) on sapphire due to reduced thermal diffusion; shorter pulses (<10 ns) for ultra-fine control but higher cost (laser physics research in Appl. Phys. A 2015 on Al2O3 ablation; Coherent laser parameters).

repetition_rate.description: Common for pulsed cleaning to achieve high throughput; 50 kHz balances speed and heat management for sapphire, where high rates risk accumulation but leverage its thermal conductivity; lower rates for heavy contamination (academic literature: J. Phys. D 2020 on pulse overlap in ceramics; manufacturer data from Trumpf).

energy_density.description: Fluence below sapphire's damage threshold (~4-6 J/cm² at 1064 nm, 20 ns pulses) for safe contaminant ablation; 1.5 J/cm² effective for industrial removal without substrate etching, given sapphire's high reflectivity (~92% transmission, low absorption); conservative for optics-grade sapphire (ablation threshold studies: Opt. Express 2017; SPIE Proc. 2019).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for precision vs. area coverage; 200 μm recommended for sapphire surface preparation, allowing fine control on flat/polished surfaces while covering larger areas efficiently; smaller for detailed work (equipment specs from Adapt Laser Systems; industry standards for optic cleaning).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for stubborn residues; 2 passes typical for complete removal on sapphire without risking cumulative damage, based on its hardness and low absorption (industry practice in watchmaking/optics case studies; Laser Cleaning Journal 2021).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or overexposure; 60% recommended for sapphire to achieve even cleaning while minimizing total energy input and HAZ, adjustable for contamination density (process optimization studies: Surf. Coat. Technol. 2019; manufacturer guidelines from CleanLaser).
